question: In a standard CMOS inverter, what happens to the propagation delay when the load capacitance is doubled?
options: Delay is halved
Delay is doubled
Delay remains the same
Delay increases by 4x

question: What is the primary purpose of floorplanning in the physical design flow?
options: To perform logic synthesis
To define the chip area, macro placement, and I/O pad locations
To fix timing violations
To run DRC checks

question: Which type of cell is used to prevent signal integrity issues caused by charge sharing on long interconnect wires?
options: Filler cell
Buffer cell
Decap cell
Tie cell

question: Setup time violation occurs when:
options: Data arrives too late at the flip-flop input before the clock edge
Data arrives too early at the flip-flop input
Clock skew is zero
Hold time is negative

question: In Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS), what is the primary goal?
options: To maximize clock frequency
To minimize clock skew and balance clock latency across all endpoints
To reduce the number of flip-flops
To eliminate all hold violations

question: What is the function of a tie-high and tie-low cell in physical design?
options: To connect floating inputs to VDD or VSS to prevent antenna violations
To balance the clock tree
To fill gaps between standard cells
To provide ESD protection

question: Which of the following is NOT a typical input to the placement stage?
options: Gate-level netlist
Floorplan DEF
Timing constraints (SDC)
Routed DEF

question: What does OCV (On-Chip Variation) account for in STA?
options: Variations in clock frequency
Process, voltage, and temperature variations across different regions of the same chip
Variations between different chip designs
Variations in the number of metal layers

question: In physical design, what is a blockage?
options: A region where routing is prohibited
A region where cell placement or routing is restricted
A type of standard cell
A clock domain crossing issue

question: What is the purpose of NDR (Non-Default Rules) in clock tree routing?
options: To reduce area utilization
To use wider wires and extra spacing for clock nets to reduce resistance and crosstalk
To increase the number of vias
To reduce the number of metal layers used

question: Which metal layers are typically preferred for clock routing and why?
options: Lower metal layers because they have lower resistance
Higher metal layers because they have lower resistance and less crosstalk
Any metal layer can be used equally
Only Metal 1 is used for clock routing

question: What is the difference between setup slack and hold slack?
options: Setup slack is always positive; hold slack is always negative
Setup slack = required time - arrival time; hold slack = arrival time - required time
They are the same metric measured at different corners
Setup slack deals with min delay; hold slack deals with max delay

question: What is an antenna violation in physical design?
options: A violation caused by electromagnetic interference between chips
Charge accumulation on metal interconnects during fabrication that can damage gate oxide
A DRC violation related to minimum spacing
A timing violation caused by long wires

question: How can antenna violations be fixed?
options: By adding more clock buffers
By inserting antenna diodes or routing jumpers to higher metal layers
By increasing the supply voltage
By reducing the number of flip-flops

question: What is useful skew in clock tree optimization?
options: Intentionally adding skew to the clock tree to improve setup/hold timing on critical paths
Unintentional clock skew caused by routing imbalance
Skew between two unrelated clock domains
Zero skew achieved after CTS

question: In a multi-corner multi-mode (MCMM) STA analysis, what does 'corner' refer to?
options: The physical corner of the chip die
A combination of PVT (Process, Voltage, Temperature) conditions
The corner pins of the chip package
The worst timing path in the design

question: Which corner is typically used for setup timing analysis?
options: Best case (fast) corner
Worst case (slow) corner
Nominal corner only
Any corner can be used equally

question: Which corner is typically used for hold timing analysis?
options: Worst case (slow) corner
Best case (fast) corner
Only the nominal corner
The corner with highest voltage

question: What is the purpose of filler cells in physical design?
options: To add extra logic functionality
To fill gaps between standard cells to maintain continuity of power rails (VDD/VSS) and n-well
To reduce dynamic power consumption
To improve clock distribution

question: What is IR drop and why is it a concern in physical design?
options: Voltage drop across the power distribution network that can cause timing failures and functional errors
Current leakage through the substrate
Voltage increase due to inductive effects
The drop in clock frequency due to routing congestion

question: What is the difference between global routing and detailed routing?
options: There is no difference; they are the same step
Global routing assigns nets to routing regions (gcells); detailed routing determines exact track and via assignments
Global routing handles clock nets; detailed routing handles signal nets
Global routing uses only lower metals; detailed routing uses only upper metals

question: What is crosstalk and what causes it?
options: Noise induced on a victim net due to capacitive coupling from a switching aggressor net running in parallel
Voltage drop across the power grid
Clock skew between two domains
Electromagnetic radiation from the chip package

question: What is the effect of crosstalk on signal timing?
options: Crosstalk has no effect on timing
It can cause both slow-down (delay increase) and speed-up (delay decrease) depending on the switching direction
It only increases delay
It only decreases delay

question: What is electromigration (EM) and which type of nets are most susceptible?
options: Movement of metal atoms due to high current density; power/ground and clock nets are most susceptible
Migration of electrons from gate to drain; signal nets are most affected
Movement of dopant atoms; I/O nets are most affected
Drift of oxide charges; all nets are equally susceptible

question: In LVS (Layout vs Schematic), what is being compared?
options: The physical layout (GDSII) extracted netlist is compared against the schematic (gate-level) netlist
Two different versions of the RTL
The floorplan against the placement
The routed design against the timing constraints

question: What is cell padding (or cell keepout margin) used for?
options: To add extra delay to cells
To add extra spacing around certain cells to avoid routing congestion or DRC violations
To increase the drive strength of cells
To reduce the leakage power of cells

question: What is the significance of the LEF file in physical design?
options: It contains the timing information of standard cells
It provides the abstract physical layout information (pins, obstructions, dimensions) of cells and technology rules
It contains the gate-level netlist
It stores the clock constraints

question: What information does the DEF file contain?
options: Timing constraints for the design
The design-specific physical layout data including die area, row definitions, component placement, and routing
Only the netlist connectivity
The liberty (.lib) timing models

question: What is the purpose of a power grid (power mesh) in physical design?
options: To route signal nets
To distribute VDD and VSS uniformly across the chip with minimal IR drop
To balance the clock tree
To create I/O ring for the chip

question: What are decap (decoupling capacitor) cells used for?
options: To increase signal propagation delay
To provide local charge storage and reduce power supply noise (dynamic IR drop)
To decouple two clock domains
To reduce the chip area

question: What is the difference between scan chain and functional mode in DFT?
options: There is no difference
In scan mode, flip-flops are connected in a shift register chain for testability; in functional mode, the design operates normally
Scan mode is faster than functional mode
Functional mode is only used during manufacturing

question: What is the impact of high utilization (e.g., >85%) on a physical design?
options: It improves timing and reduces area
It leads to routing congestion, difficulty in timing closure, and increased DRC violations
It has no impact on the design
It only affects power consumption

question: What is AOCV (Advanced OCV) and how is it different from flat OCV?
options: AOCV is the same as flat OCV with a different name
AOCV applies stage-based and distance-based derating factors instead of a single flat derate, providing more realistic and less pessimistic analysis
AOCV only applies to hold analysis
AOCV eliminates the need for multi-corner analysis

question: In physical design, what is a halo (or keepout region) around a macro?
options: A region of extra metal shielding
An exclusion zone around a macro where no standard cells can be placed
A power ring around the macro
A clock buffer zone around the macro

question: What is the formula for dynamic power consumption in CMOS?
options: P = V × I
P = α × C_L × V_DD² × f
P = I²R
P = V_DD × I_leakage

question: Which technique is used to reduce leakage power in modern designs?
options: Increasing the supply voltage
Using multi-threshold voltage (multi-Vt) cells - HVT for non-critical paths, LVT for critical paths
Removing all buffer cells
Using only minimum-width transistors

question: What is the purpose of an ECO (Engineering Change Order) in physical design?
options: To redesign the entire chip from scratch
To make incremental functional or timing fixes to the design without doing a full re-implementation
To change the technology node
To update the PDK library

question: What is congestion in physical design and how does it affect the design?
options: Congestion refers to excessive power consumption
Congestion occurs when routing demand exceeds available routing resources in a region, leading to DRC violations and detours
Congestion only affects clock nets
Congestion is a placement metric that has no effect on routing

question: In STA, what is a false path?
options: A path that has negative slack
A timing path that exists structurally in the netlist but can never be sensitized during actual circuit operation
A path with zero delay
A path that only exists during scan mode

question: What is the difference between a latch and a flip-flop in terms of timing behavior?
options: There is no difference
A latch is level-sensitive (transparent when enable is active) while a flip-flop is edge-triggered (captures data only on clock edge)
A flip-flop is faster than a latch
A latch uses more power than a flip-flop
